entry.title: Stone Butch Blues
entry.content:
by Leslie Feinberg

✮✮✮✮✮

I don't think this review box could possibly hold all that I learned from this book. (Must write more later)

entry.title: The Amazing Adventures of Kavalier & Clay
entry.summary:
by Michael Chabon

✮✮✮✮✮

I really liked the ideas behind [b:The Yiddish Policemen's Union|16703|The Yiddish Policemen's Union|Michael Chabon|http://photo.goodreads.com/books/1178032098s/16703.jpg|95855], but this one was honestly much, much better. The characters, the plot, the place, the time... it all comes together …
entry.content:
by Michael Chabon

✮✮✮✮✮

I really liked the ideas behind [b:The Yiddish Policemen's Union|16703|The Yiddish Policemen's Union|Michael Chabon|http://photo.goodreads.com/books/1178032098s/16703.jpg|95855], but this one was honestly much, much better. The characters, the plot, the place, the time... it all comes together.

1 Nov 2010 -- Rereading because Sam and Mel both just finished it and I want to discuss it with them. Read about 150 pages in the first night. I forgot how awesome and utterly readable this book is.

entry.title: Anything Goes
entry.content:
by John Barrowman, Carole E. Barrowman

no star rating

Silly, but fast and fun.

by Patrick McCabe

entry.title: Normal: Transsexual CEOs, Crossdressing Cops, and Hermaphrodites with Attitude
entry.summary:
by Amy Bloom, Sigrid Estrada, J.K. Lambert, Allison Saltzman

✮✮✮✮☆

I read this in 2008 and I think it was among the first dozen books I read about transness, simply because it was on the HQ shelf in the college library. I don't recall a thing about it and I'm …
entry.content:
by Amy Bloom, Sigrid Estrada, J.K. Lambert, Allison Saltzman

✮✮✮✮☆

I read this in 2008 and I think it was among the first dozen books I read about transness, simply because it was on the HQ shelf in the college library. I don't recall a thing about it and I'm sure it wasn't anything special, but I'll respect my past self giving it 4/5 since I have no plans to reread.

entry.title: Pride and Prejudice
entry.summary:
by Jane Austen, Anna Quindlen

✮✮✮✮☆

I was surprised how much I liked this. I never thought it was my kind of book, but Sam told me to read it, and she was right. I've seen all the screen adaptations-- most of the humor really doesn't translate well to visual, so …
entry.content:
by Jane Austen, Anna Quindlen

✮✮✮✮☆

I was surprised how much I liked this. I never thought it was my kind of book, but Sam told me to read it, and she was right. I've seen all the screen adaptations-- most of the humor really doesn't translate well to visual, so I'm not sure what the attraction of making a movie out of it is!

entry.title: Renegade (Star Trek: The Original Series #55)
entry.content:
by Gene DeWeese

✮✮☆☆☆

I got this for 50 cents at a library booksale. Short, utilitarian, plot-based sci-fi. Not badly written at all, but not thrilling either.

entry.title: Me Talk Pretty One Day
entry.summary:
by David Sedaris

✮✮✮☆☆

I didn't mind this book at all-- in fact, I read it cover to cover in a few hours. It was mostly interesting and well-written, and it definitely passed the time. But I'd been led to expect that Sedaris was piss-your-pants funny, and it turned out not …
entry.content:
by David Sedaris

✮✮✮☆☆

I didn't mind this book at all-- in fact, I read it cover to cover in a few hours. It was mostly interesting and well-written, and it definitely passed the time. But I'd been led to expect that Sedaris was piss-your-pants funny, and it turned out not to be the case for me. Guess Bill Bryson is secure in his place!

entry.title: Snow Crash
entry.summary:
by Neal Stephenson

✮✮✮✮☆

I quite enjoyed this-- it's not my favorite, or even my favorite [a:Neal Stephenson|545|Neal Stephenson|http://photo.goodreads.com/authors/1192826259p2/545.jpg], but it certainly captures a lot of the things I enjoy about his writing style. Stephenson does really good near-future sci-fi …
entry.content:
by Neal Stephenson

✮✮✮✮☆

I quite enjoyed this-- it's not my favorite, or even my favorite [a:Neal Stephenson|545|Neal Stephenson|http://photo.goodreads.com/authors/1192826259p2/545.jpg], but it certainly captures a lot of the things I enjoy about his writing style. Stephenson does really good near-future sci-fi because he excels at taking something from our own time and going crazy with it, but at the same time keeping it detailed and sprawling and very realistic. In the case of [b:Snow Crash|830|Snow Crash|Neal Stephenson|http://photo.goodreads.com/books/1157396730s/830.jpg|493634], all you have to do is accept one possibly science-fictional idea in order for the rest of the story and world to fall into place with a resounding crash.

Near the end where they're author-dumping philosophy of religion... I can even agree with the line of thinking to an extent, but the part where Judaism is summoned up and just as quickly dismissed as 'a good attempt for the time, but outdated and replaced by Christianity' felt like a punch in the stomach. That's a pretty good simplification of the way it looks from the outside, but it's something Jews hear all the time, as if Judaism is made irrelevant and pointless by its eventually more popular offshoot.

Almost as a side note, I was entertained to notice the similarities of Hiro's "Earth" program to Google Earth. I wonder whether Google got their inspiration from the book, or if it's just an obvious idea. (Probably more the latter.)

entry.title: Ella Minnow Pea: A Novel in Letters
entry.summary:
by Mark Dunn

✮✮✮✮✮

I've been looking for Ibid: A Life by this author for years. Today, I finally found it-- but it didn't look as good as I thought. Fortunately, this gem was sitting right next to it on the shelf.

Ella Minnow Pea is a relatively simple little satire …
entry.content:
by Mark Dunn

✮✮✮✮✮

I've been looking for Ibid: A Life by this author for years. Today, I finally found it-- but it didn't look as good as I thought. Fortunately, this gem was sitting right next to it on the shelf.

Ella Minnow Pea is a relatively simple little satire about fanaticism told with a wonderful sense of energy and fun. As the story progresses, it also acquires darker undercurrents of divorce, madness, and death, flowing tragicomically from the extension of one ludicrous idea. I'd almost recommend it as a children's book, but the prose is honestly difficult (although always entertaining) and especially toward the end it becomes nearly impossible to read. At slightly over 200 pages, it's short-- I read it in a sitting-- so give it a shot! As for my copy, I'm torn between circulating it among all my friends as soon as humanly possible and selfishly clinging to it and reading it over again.

entry.title: Memoirs of a Geisha
entry.summary:
by Arthur Golden

✮✮✮☆☆

The most fascinating thing about this book was easily the level of historical detail it incorporated. The story and characters didn't always captivate me, but somehow I had trouble putting it down and not reading one more paragraph (which without fail became two more chapters). The language …
entry.content:
by Arthur Golden

✮✮✮☆☆

The most fascinating thing about this book was easily the level of historical detail it incorporated. The story and characters didn't always captivate me, but somehow I had trouble putting it down and not reading one more paragraph (which without fail became two more chapters). The language and metaphors were beautiful and only added to the presence of the time and place.

The ending took me by surprise; I could hardly finish the last couple chapters because I just knew it was going to end horribly. I still haven't decided whether it fit or not. The stream-reaching-ocean metaphor is somehow not very Western, where I think we don't (reflexively, at least) believe in fate.

entry.title: Ravens in the Storm: A Personal History of the 1960s Anti-War Movement
entry.summary:
by Carl Oglesby

✮✮✮✮☆

I picked up Ravens at the library on the strength of its cover and didn't managed to put down. An enthralling memoir of the late 1960s and early 1970s. I learned so much history reading this book: of people and events that I had heard of but …
entry.content:
by Carl Oglesby

✮✮✮✮☆

I picked up Ravens at the library on the strength of its cover and didn't managed to put down. An enthralling memoir of the late 1960s and early 1970s. I learned so much history reading this book: of people and events that I had heard of but didn't know in detail, or didn't know were contemporary. I now intend to pick up some of the other books Oglesby suggests.

The book is not perfect-- for one, as with all autobiographical works, I'd be interested to hear whether the people who appear in the book feel it's faithful to events. For another, it's occasionally repetitive, to the point that entire sentences were somehow transplanted wholesale to the third part of the book. Each chapter is organized chronologically, but the book as a whole jumps back and forth a bit, which can get confusing. This would be more justifiable if the theme of each chapter was tighter.

But don't let my nitpicks get in the way. If you've ever had any interest in the anti-war movement, this is a fascinating introduction and analysis, spiced with personal anecdoted that run the gamut from hilarious to tragic. I am much too young to remember the '60s, but I always wished I could have been there; thus, I'd be interested to hear what people who were there have to say about Ravens.

entry.title: The Life and Times of the Thunderbolt Kid
entry.content:
by Bill Bryson

✮✮✮✮☆

Like all Bryson books, both hilarious and surprisingly informative. My one complaint is that towards the end he begins to cave in to a less interesting and insightful nostalgia. Still, tons of fun and wholeheartedly recommend to all who like to giggle uncontrollably!

entry.title: Practical Magic (Practical Magic, #1)
entry.summary:
by Alice Hoffman

✮✮✮✮☆

I think the thing I liked most about this book was the way it was written. It has the most wonderful, quietly magical atmosphere, laced with elaborate, true-to-life superstitions and character insights. I read the first third of the book long before I went back and finished …
entry.content:
by Alice Hoffman

✮✮✮✮☆

I think the thing I liked most about this book was the way it was written. It has the most wonderful, quietly magical atmosphere, laced with elaborate, true-to-life superstitions and character insights. I read the first third of the book long before I went back and finished it, so I didn't even keep track of the plot all that well.

What it boils down to is that I'm not sure I can describe this book well but I really enjoyed reading it.

entry.title: A Brief History of Time
entry.content:
by Stephen W. Hawking

✮✮✮✮☆

An very well-written book-- highly accessible for the layman, in depth without being unmanageable.

entry.title: Arrows of the Queen (Heralds of Valdemar, #1)
entry.content:
by Mercedes Lackey

✮✮☆☆☆

Another reread. Mercedes Lackey is one of those authors you have to read in your adolescent years to appreciate. Arrows is (mostly) well-written-- I began to notice some awkward spots this time around-- and certainly fun, but far from great literature.

entry.title: Catch-22
entry.content:
by Joseph Heller

✮✮✮✮✮

Finally finished it today-- about all I can say about this at the moment is !!!!!! Both hilarious and tragic, occasionally at the same time. One of my new favorites.

entry.title: Kitty and the Midnight Hour (Kitty Norville, #1)
entry.content:
by Carrie Vaughn

✮✮✮☆☆

Okay, I admit it, it's a reread. A nice, lightweight novel about a Denver werewolf who starts a talk-radio show for the supernatural community. Nicely written; enjoyable characters. I admit that the Denver references were part of the draw!

entry.title: Komarr (Vorkosigan Saga, #11)
entry.summary:
by Lois McMaster Bujold

✮✮✮✮☆

The first Vorkosigan book I read. <3

(February 2008) Okay, Mel lent this to me and I read it in less than a week. Possibly this was me being starved for sf+f, but the book deserves serious credit. It grabbed me almost immediately, and Miles …
entry.content:
by Lois McMaster Bujold

✮✮✮✮☆

The first Vorkosigan book I read. <3

(February 2008) Okay, Mel lent this to me and I read it in less than a week. Possibly this was me being starved for sf+f, but the book deserves serious credit. It grabbed me almost immediately, and Miles makes me happy inside. I am so glad there are about a bajillion more of these books-- if only I didn't have homework!

(August 2017) I found myself not entirely comfortable with the arguments about of when it's time to end the fighting and accept defeat. The answer is... the status quo is fine, because the characters we are invested in are on the top of the wheel at the moment? The difference between a rebellion and a revolution is which side you're on.

entry.title: Black Powder War (Temeraire, #3)
entry.summary:
by Naomi Novik

✮✮✮☆☆

The Temeraire books are great, far surpassing the rather hackneyed Pern books that define the "dragon rider" genre and leaving books like Eragon in the Kuiper Belt. Novik is bursting with new ideas, and while a few of them fall a little flatter than others, the books …
entry.content:
by Naomi Novik

✮✮✮☆☆

The Temeraire books are great, far surpassing the rather hackneyed Pern books that define the "dragon rider" genre and leaving books like Eragon in the Kuiper Belt. Novik is bursting with new ideas, and while a few of them fall a little flatter than others, the books are still utterly original.

The books take place in a world best described as "the Napoleonic wars-- with dragons!" That doesn't do much to describe the deep and highly interesting characters, the 19th-century mannerisms, and the general "age of sail" feeling to the series. Temeraire himself is a delight, and promises to remain so.

entry.title: Einstein: His Life and Universe
entry.content:
by Walter Isaacson

no star rating

entry.title: Mirror Dance (Vorkosigan Saga, #8)
entry.summary:
by Lois McMaster Bujold

no star rating

I cannot read this entire book again. Mark's half of the story triggers my embarrassment squick something awful (he's terrified everyone hates him for fucking up, and he's basically right), and the last third of the book is incredibly graphic and inventive torture …
entry.content:
by Lois McMaster Bujold

no star rating

I cannot read this entire book again. Mark's half of the story triggers my embarrassment squick something awful (he's terrified everyone hates him for fucking up, and he's basically right), and the last third of the book is incredibly graphic and inventive torture scenes. Or maybe it only felt that way when I was bravely trying to skim it. When I want to pick it up, I usually skip around to the Miles bits.

Maybe this is the reason I don't enjoy reading about Mark. Obviously lots of people disagree with me, though, and that's cool!

entry.title: The Bacchae
entry.content:
by Euripides, Kenneth McLeish, Frederic Raphael

no star rating

entry.title: Yu+Me: dream Volume 1 (Yu+Me: dream, #1)
entry.content:
by Megan Rose Gedris

no star rating

I used to read this online! Wow, maybe time for a revisit.
